End-to-end guide

QE Automation Framework

A TypeScript Playwright framework built on Page Object Model + Component Object Model, dependency-injected fixtures, environment-driven config, and Allure/HTML reporting. This is the complete walkthrough — every feature, the steps to use it, and what it actually looks like on screen.

Node.js 20+ Playwright 1.61 TypeScript Allure 3

01

What you're working with

The shape of the framework before you touch a single command.

This repo automates a browser application end to end: UI flows via Page Objects, API calls via a typed client, and everything wired together through Playwright fixtures so a test never constructs its own dependencies. Every run produces two independent reports — Playwright's own HTML report, and a much richer Allure report with named steps, screenshots, and video evidence attached automatically.

Page Object Model
UI locators and actions live in src/pages/, never inline in a test.
Fixtures
Every page, service, and helper is injected — tests destructure what they need instead of calling new.
Multi-environment
Four config tiers (qa/stage/uat/prod) switched with one env var.
13 test disciplines
From unit tests to chaos tests, each with a working reference script.
AI-assisted
Five Copilot chatmodes, two auto-loaded skills, and an MCP server for programmatic control.

02

Quick start

Five commands from a clean clone to a passing suite.

  1. Install dependencies

    Also wires up the Husky pre-commit hook (lint-staged runs ESLint + Prettier on every commit).

    npm install
  2. Install browsers

    Downloads Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with their OS dependencies.

    npm run install:browsers
  3. Configure an environment

    Copy the template and fill in real values. .env.qa already ships with safe local defaults pointed at the bundled demo app.

    cp .env.example .env.qa
  4. Run the suite

    npm test starts the bundled demo server automatically via Playwright's webServer option — nothing to run in a second terminal.

    npm test
  5. Open the report 
    npx playwright show-report reports/html

No real target app required to start. A minimal demo app ships in demo/ — a login form, a dashboard, and an animated component — so the smoke/sanity/e2e suites and every sample script run immediately, with zero external dependencies.

03

Architecture

Where things live, and why.

src/pages/ sits directly under src/ because it's what tests touch most. Everything else — the framework "engine" — lives under src/core/, grouped by role:

src/core/ui

BasePage, reusable Components, Locators — everything that touches the raw Playwright Page API.

src/core/api

ApiClient, business Services, optional multi-database clients.

src/core/runtime

Fixtures, GlobalSetup/Teardown, console + failure capture hooks.

src/core/data

Test data, Fluent builders, domain models, enums.

src/core/config

The Config singleton, constants, Winston logger.

src/core/shared

Interfaces, exceptions, *Utils static helpers, validations.

The rule of thumb

  • Tests only do Arrange / Act / Assert, using fixtures — never new PageObject(page).
  • Page objects only do locators, actions, and navigation — zero assertions.
  • Assertions live in ValidationHelpers or a plain expect() in the test.
  • Nothing is hardcoded — URLs, credentials, and timeouts always route through Config or src/core/config/constants/.

04

src/core, file by file

What's actually inside each of the six groups — every file, real.

ui

src/core/ui/

Everything that touches the raw Playwright Page/Locator API. BasePage is the base every page object extends; BaseComponent is the same idea scoped to a root locator, for reusable UI fragments. LocatorFactory and CommonLocators steer every locator toward getByRole/getByLabel/getByText — never CSS, never XPath.

base/BasePage.ts               click, fill, hover, drag-drop, upload, download,
                                wait*, screenshot, alert handling — 25+ actions
components/BaseComponent.ts    scopes a component to a root Locator
components/HeaderComponent.ts  logo, nav, profile/logout buttons
components/NavigationMenu.ts
components/TableComponent.ts   role="row"/"cell" driven, works on any table
components/Pagination.ts       next/previous, page indicator text
components/ToastMessage.ts     role="status" notifications
components/CommonDialog.ts     role="dialog" confirm/cancel modals
locators/LocatorFactory.ts     byRoleAndName, byLabel, byPlaceholder, byText, byTestId
locators/CommonLocators.ts     selectors shared across more than one page

Every BasePage action follows the same shape — wait for the element, then act, with debug logging on every call:

async click(locator: Locator): Promise<void> {
  Logger.debug(`Clicking locator: ${locator}`);
  await locator.waitFor({ state: 'visible' });
  await locator.click();
}

api

src/core/api/

Backend I/O. ApiClient wraps GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE with built-in status/JSON assertions; services/ wrap it again with domain methods (AuthService.login(), JobService.createJob()); database/ is an opt-in multi-driver layer behind one factory.

ApiClient.ts                       GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE + expectStatus/getJson
services/AuthService.ts
services/JobService.ts
database/DatabaseClientFactory.ts  create(DatabaseType.X, options) -> IDatabaseClient
database/IDatabaseClient.ts
database/PostgresClient.ts         pg — not installed by default
database/MySQLClient.ts            mysql2 — not installed by default
database/MsSqlClient.ts            mssql — not installed by default
database/OracleClient.ts           oracledb — not installed by default

runtime

src/core/runtime/

The wiring that makes dependency injection work. fixtures.ts is the single file every test imports from; hooks/ run setup/teardown and failure capture; listeners/ attach console/network logging and the custom pass/fail summary reporter.

fixtures/fixtures.ts             every injectable: pages, services, config, logger…
hooks/GlobalSetup.ts             creates report dirs, writes Allure environment.properties
hooks/GlobalTeardown.ts
hooks/TestLifecycleHooks.ts      captureOnFailure — screenshot + last 50 log lines
listeners/PageEventListener.ts   console/network logging, attached per test
listeners/CustomReporter.ts      structured pass/fail/skip/duration summary via Winston

data

src/core/data/

Test data and the shapes it takes. builders/ are Fluent builders (.withEmail().withPassword().build()); testdata/ holds static JSON/CSV plus typed endpoint/payload catalogs; models/ and enums/ are the shared domain vocabulary.

builders/UserBuilder.ts
builders/JobBuilder.ts
testdata/urls.testdata.ts
testdata/apiEndpoints.testdata.ts
testdata/payloads.testdata.ts
testdata/users.testdata.ts
testdata/sample-users.json / .csv
models/User.model.ts / Job.model.ts / ApiResponse.model.ts
enums/BrowserType.ts, Environment.ts, DatabaseType.ts, UserRole.ts,
      Status.ts, Country.ts, Language.ts, ApplicationModule.ts

config

src/core/config/

Config is a Singleton — typed getters over whichever .env.<tier> loaded, with sane fallback defaults so the demo app works with zero setup. Nothing in the framework reads process.env directly outside this file.

Config.ts                    environment, baseUrl, apiBaseUrl, adminUsername/Password,
                              headless, retries, browser, dbType…
constants/EnvKeys.ts         the .env variable names, as constants
constants/Routes.ts          /login, /dashboard, /users, /products, /reports
constants/Timeouts.ts        DEFAULT, SHORT, MEDIUM, LONG, PAGE_LOAD, API_REQUEST…
constants/Messages.ts, Roles.ts, ReportNames.ts, FilePaths.ts
logger/Logger.ts             Winston — daily file + error file + console (dev only)

shared

src/core/shared/

Cross-cutting support with no opinion about pages, API, or data specifically. This is also where every *Utils static helper lives — 16 of them, plus the two purpose-built ones added alongside this guide: AllureUtils and AccessibilityUtils.

validations/ValidationHelpers.ts   verifyText, verifyUrl, verifyElementVisible…
exceptions/                        APIError, ConfigurationError, DatabaseError,
                                    ElementError, ValidationError, FrameworkError
helpers/TestDataProvider.ts        readJson/readCsv/readExcel from data/testdata
helpers/RetryHelper.ts, ReportAttachmentHelper.ts
interfaces/IConfig.ts
utils/AllureUtils.ts               step/steps/attachScreenshot/attachVideo/setCategory
utils/AccessibilityUtils.ts        runAxeScan(page) — shared axe-core defaults
utils/BrowserUtils.ts              builds the 5 Playwright projects
utils/ScreenshotUtils.ts, DateUtils, JsonUtils, CSVUtils, ExcelUtils, FileUtils,
     NetworkUtils, RandomDataUtils, StorageUtils, DownloadUtils, UploadUtils,
     EncryptionUtils, ZipUtils, APIUtils, WaitUtils
tools/dev-server.js, compare.js    the demo server + visual-diff CLI (see below)

Every one of these is a plain static-method class named XxxUtils — that's the convention. Adding a new cross-cutting helper means adding one file here, not a new pattern.

05

The bundled demo app

A real target to point every example in this guide at.

Served from demo/ by a tiny static server (src/core/tools/dev-server.js) on http://127.0.0.1:3000. Playwright starts and stops it automatically around every test run — you never launch it by hand unless you're exploring manually with npm run dev:server.

06

The four page objects

All of src/pages/ — locators, methods, and what each one is standing in for.

LoginPage.ts

usernameInput · passwordInput · loginButton · errorMessage · header

Composes one component (HeaderComponent). Methods: navigate(), enterUsername(), enterPassword(), clickLogin(), login() (does all three), getErrorMessage(), getErrorMessageLocator().

DemoPage.ts

animatedBox · animateButton · navButton

The simplest page in the repo — no composed components. Methods: goToDemo(), clickAnimateButton(), clickNavButton(), sampleAnimationFrames(durationMs).

WeSendCVPage.ts

emailInput · passwordInput · submitButton · navigationMenu

The one page object that targets a real external site (wesendcv.com) rather than the bundled demo. Methods: goToWeSendCV(url), login(), getNavigationMenu().

DashboardPage.ts

6 composed components + saveButton

The reference example for the Component Object Model — see below.

Composition in practice

DashboardPage doesn't declare its own table/pagination/toast locators — it composes six independent components, each already tested and reused elsewhere:

export class DashboardPage extends BasePage {
  public readonly header: HeaderComponent;
  public readonly navigationMenu: NavigationMenu;
  public readonly jobsTable: TableComponent;
  public readonly pagination: Pagination;
  public readonly toast: ToastMessage;
  public readonly logoutDialog: CommonDialog;

  constructor(page: Page) {
    super(page);
    this.header = new HeaderComponent(page);
    this.jobsTable = new TableComponent(page, page.getByRole('table', { name: 'Jobs' }));
    this.pagination = new Pagination(page, page.getByRole('navigation', { name: 'Pagination' }));
    this.toast = new ToastMessage(page);
    this.logoutDialog = new CommonDialog(page);
  }

  async saveChanges(): Promise<void> {
    await this.click(this.saveButton);
  }
}

A component never knows which page it's on. TableComponent is generic over any role="table" — it's used by DashboardPage here, but nothing about it is dashboard-specific. That's what makes it reusable instead of copy-pasted.

07

Running tests

Every command you'll actually type.

By category

CommandRuns
npm testEverything, across all five browser projects
npm run test:smokeCritical-path smoke tests
npm run test:sanityQuick PR sanity checks
npm run test:regressionFull regression suite
npm run test:e2eEnd-to-end user flows
npm run test:apiAPI-only tests

Browser & environment

CommandEffect
npx playwright test --project=chromiumOne browser only
BROWSER=firefox npm testPin the whole run to one engine
ENVIRONMENT=stage npm testLoad .env.stage instead of .env.qa

Debugging

ModeCommand
Watch it run headednpm run test:headed
Step through with the Inspectornpm run test:debug
Interactive UI modenpm run test:ui

Five browser projects run by default: chromium, firefox, webkit, plus Mobile Chrome and Mobile Safari emulation — configured once in BrowserUtils.buildProjects(), not repeated per test.

08

Tutorial: write a script the right way

Best practices first, then one real script built and run from scratch.

Best practices, as code

The Coding Standards below aren't a style preference — each one exists because the alternative broke something. Here's what following (and breaking) each one actually looks like:

Do

  • Extend BasePage for every action
  • Import { test, expect } from fixtures.ts
  • Destructure fixtures: async ({ dashboardPage })
  • Assert via ValidationHelpers or expect() in the test
  • Pull values from Config / constants/
  • getByRole / getByLabel / getByTestId

Don't

  • Call page.click(...) directly in a page object
  • Import from @playwright/test in a spec
  • new DashboardPage(page) inside a test
  • Assert inside a page object method
  • Hardcode a URL, password, or timeout number
  • CSS selectors, and never XPath

One rule earns its own line: a page object that asserts is the single most common review comment in this codebase. If a method name starts to want a toBe/toHave inside it, that logic belongs in the test or in ValidationHelpers instead.

Worked example: dashboard save confirmation

Goal: verify that clicking Save Changes shows the "Saved successfully" toast — using only what already exists (dashboardPage is already a registered fixture, saveChanges() and toast already exist on it), so this is purely a new spec file.

  1. Check what's already available

    Before writing anything, look at the page object. DashboardPage already exposes saveChanges() and a composed toast — nothing new to build.

  2. Write the spec

    Categorize it with AllureUtils.setCategory, name each phase with AllureUtils.step, and attach proof with AllureUtils.attachScreenshot — on pass, not just on failure.

    import { test, expect } from '../../src/core/runtime/fixtures/fixtures';
import { AllureUtils } from '../../src/core/shared/utils/AllureUtils';

test.describe('Dashboard Save Confirmation', () => {
  test.beforeEach(async () => {
    await AllureUtils.setCategory('Dashboard', 'Save Changes', AllureUtils.Severity.NORMAL);
  });

  test('shows a success toast after saving changes', async ({ dashboardPage, page }) => {
    await AllureUtils.step('Navigate to the dashboard', async () => {
      await dashboardPage.navigate();
    });

    await AllureUtils.step('Click Save Changes', async () => {
      await dashboardPage.saveChanges();
    });

    await AllureUtils.step('Verify the success toast appears', async () => {
      await dashboardPage.toast.waitForVisible();
      expect(await dashboardPage.toast.getMessage()).toContain('Saved successfully');
    });

    await AllureUtils.attachScreenshot(page, 'Toast confirmation');
  });
});
  3. Run it 
    npx playwright test tests/smoke/tutorial-dashboard-save.spec.ts --project=chromium
    Running 1 test using 1 worker ✓ 1 [chromium] › tutorial-dashboard-save.spec.ts:9:7 › Dashboard Save Confirmation › shows a success toast after saving changes (961ms) 1 passed (2.8s)

    That's the actual, unedited output from running this exact file against this exact repo.

  4. Check the evidence

    Regenerate the Allure report (npm run allurereport) — the run now has its own named steps and a screenshot attached, in the same style as every example in the AllureUtils section above.

Nothing here required touching a page object. That's the point of the pattern: most new tests are new specs against existing fixtures. You only add to src/pages/ or src/core/ when a genuinely new interaction doesn't exist yet — see the next section for that case.

When you do need a new page object

  1. Extend BasePage

    Locators + actions + navigation only — no assertions, ever.

    export class DemoPage extends BasePage {
  private readonly animateButton: Locator;

  constructor(page: Page) {
    super(page);
    this.animateButton = this.page.getByRole('button', { name: /animate box/i });
  }

  async clickAnimateButton(): Promise<void> {
    await this.click(this.animateButton);
  }
}
  2. Register it as a fixture

    In src/core/runtime/fixtures/fixtures.ts — this is what makes it injectable instead of hand-instantiated.

    demoPage: async ({ page }, use) => {
  await use(new DemoPage(page));
},
  3. Write the spec, run it, done

    Same shape as the worked example above — fixtures in, AllureUtils around every phase, no new, no assertions in the page object.

09

13 sample scripts, one per discipline

tests/zsampleScript/ — a working, passing reference for every kind of test this framework supports.

Each file is a real spec, not pseudo-code — it runs against the demo app as part of a plain npm test, and demonstrates the pattern in miniature.

npx playwright test tests/zsampleScript                               # all 13
npx playwright test tests/zsampleScript/security-tests.sample.spec.ts  # one category

unit-tests.sample.spec.ts

chaos-tests.sample.spec.ts

10

Reporting

Two independent reports come out of every run — a fast native one, and a detailed one.

Playwright's own HTML report

Zero setup — every run writes it. Best for a quick pass/fail scan right after a local run.

Allure — the detailed report

Raw results are written automatically on every run (the allure-playwright reporter is already registered). Generating and opening the static site is a separate step:

npm run allurereport   # generate + open in one command
# — or, separately —
npm run allure:generate
npm run allure:open

11

Named steps and evidence, on every run

Not just on failure — AllureUtils attaches proof for every test, pass or fail.

Playwright's own screenshot/video capture is failure-only by config. AllureUtils (src/core/shared/utils/AllureUtils.ts) is the fix: a small façade that wraps allure-js-commons so a spec writes one line instead of five, and gets a named, collapsible step with a real screenshot inside it regardless of outcome.

The API

AllureUtils.setCategory(epic, feature, severity?)

Sets the labels a test is grouped and prioritized by in one call, instead of three separate allure.epic/feature/severity calls.

AllureUtils.step(name, fn)

Wraps a block of actions as one named, collapsible step — the building block for everything else here.

AllureUtils.steps(name, ...actions)

Same idea, for a flat list of actions run in sequence — skips writing async () => { await a(); await b(); } at the call site.

AllureUtils.attachScreenshot(page, name?)

Captures and attaches a PNG, regardless of pass or fail — this is what produced both screenshots above.

AllureUtils.attachJson(name, data)

Attaches any object as pretty-printed JSON — request payloads, computed values, anything worth inspecting later.

AllureUtils.attachVideo(page, context, name?)

Attaches the full recording for a test. Requires a context created with recordVideo set — see e2e-tests.sample.spec.ts for the complete pattern.

AllureUtils.parameter(name, value)

Shows a labelled value on the report page — durations, computed flags, anything worth surfacing without a full attachment.

Regenerate after every run. Raw results write automatically, but the static site does not rebuild itself — run npm run allurereport (or allure:generate) after the tests finish, every time, or you'll be looking at a stale report.

12

Visual regression

Pixel-level diffing, with automatic baseline bootstrap.

src/core/tools/compare.js diffs two PNGs with pixelmatch and fails past a configurable threshold. If no baseline exists yet, it creates one from the current screenshot and exits clean — the first run is never a false failure.

npm run compare -- baseline.png current.png diff.png --threshold=0.03

The same technique is also available as a typed, importable method — ScreenshotUtils.compare() in src/core/shared/utils/ — for specs that want the check without shelling out to a script.

13

Chatmodes & skills

Five personas you switch into, two behaviors Copilot applies on its own.

NameKindBest for
🩺 HealerchatmodeDiagnose and fix a failing test
📋 PlannerchatmodeDraft a test plan before writing code
⚙️ GeneratorchatmodeWrite spec files and page objects
🔌 API TestingchatmodeScaffold API and Pact contract tests
📝 Manual TestingchatmodeGenerate a manual QA checklist
🔍 Code Reviewskill · autoAudit POM compliance, security, anti-patterns
🛠️ Test Debuggingskill · autoSystematic failure triage

Activating a chatmode in VS Code

  1. Open Copilot Chat

    Cmd/Ctrl+Alt+I

  2. Pick from the dropdown

    Or skip it and prefix your message directly: @healer Fix the failing test in tests/wesendcv.spec.ts

  3. Skills need nothing

    Just ask a normal question — "review this spec for POM compliance" loads the Code Review skill automatically.

14

MCP server

Let an AI client run your tests as tool calls instead of shell commands.

mcp/mcp-server.ts exposes this framework over the Model Context Protocol (stdio transport). Any MCP-compatible client — Claude Desktop, Claude Code, VS Code — can launch it as a subprocess.

npm run mcp:server
ToolArgumentsDoes
run_playwright_testtestFile, project?, headed?Runs one spec file
run_all_tests—Runs the full suite
get_test_report—Lists playwright-report/ contents
Client config
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright-ai-agent": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["tsx", "mcp/mcp-server.ts"],
      "cwd": "/absolute/path/to/repo"
    }
  }
}

15

Running in Docker

Same suite, containerized — for parity with CI.

docker compose up --build

Reports and results are bind-mounted back to the host, so reports/ is populated exactly as a local run would leave it. Override the default command to run a specific suite:

docker compose run --rm playwright-tests npm run test:smoke

16

CI/CD

What happens on every push and PR.

.github/workflows/ci.yml runs on every push and pull request against main:

checkout → setup-node(20) → npm ci → lint → typecheck
  → install:browsers → npm test → allure:generate → upload artifact

A manual workflow_dispatch trigger lets you pick the environment and browser for an on-demand run from the Actions tab.

17

Troubleshooting

The fast paths, in order of how often you'll need them.

A test failed

Check reports/html first — screenshot, video, and trace are already attached, plus the last 50 log lines.

Need to step through it

npm run test:debug for the Inspector, or npm run test:ui for the interactive runner.

Allure report looks stale

Regenerate — it never rebuilds itself. npm run allurereport.

Browser install issues

npm run install:browsers — safe to re-run any time.

Generated from the live repository — every command, file path, and screenshot on this page was run and captured directly against this codebase.
